coredata.dc:title: Nanochannel regulation of graphene quantum dots composite membranes via electrospray assisted self-assembly method
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hailiang
Sun, Yumin
Cheng, Shiyun
Qin, Yang
Feng, Xianshe
Huang, Qinglin
Chen, Kaikai
coredata.dc:description: Nanochannel construction and regulation of graphene-based composite films are critical to the efficient separation performance. We report on a novel approach to preparing porous graphene-based composite films based on electrospray assisted self-assembly method by intercalating diamine monomers between graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanosheets. Four kinds of diamine monomers were chosen as the bridging agents to fine tune the nanochannels between GQDs nanosheets. The physicochemical structures of as-prepared GQDs composite membranes were further optimized by post-crosslinking. The results showed that the covalent reactions of GQDs nanosheets with diamine monomers were occurred simultaneously between parallel and perpendicular GQDs nanosheets in the membrane preparation process, which constructed the three-dimensional space architecture of the GQDs composite membrane. The diamine monomers with different structures and molecular weights could change the loose structures of the separation layers, which endowed the GQDs composite membranes with the adjustable pore size and better hydrophilicity, further affecting their dye desalination performance. With the increase of the molecular weight of diamine monomers, the pore size of GQDs composite membranes increased slightly from 3.4 nm (GQDs-EDA) to 3.8 nm (GQDs-PPD), while the dye rejection decreased slightly. This study provided an insight into the uniform self-assembly of GQDs into nanofilms to fine tune the nanochannel structure.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanochannel construction and regulation of graphene-based composite films are critical to the efficient separation performance. We report on a novel approach to preparing porous graphene-based composite films based on electrospray assisted self-assembly method by intercalating diamine monomers between graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanosheets. Four kinds of diamine monomers were chosen as the bridging agents to fine tune the nanochannels between GQDs nanosheets. The physicochemical structures of as-prepared GQDs composite membranes were further optimized by post-crosslinking. The results showed that the covalent reactions of GQDs nanosheets with diamine monomers were occurred simultaneously between parallel and perpendicular GQDs nanosheets in the membrane preparation process, which constructed the three-dimensional space architecture of the GQDs composite membrane. The diamine monomers with different structures and molecular weights could change the loose structures of the separation layers, which endowed the GQDs composite membranes with the adjustable pore size and better hydrophilicity, further affecting their dye desalination performance. With the increase of the molecular weight of diamine monomers, the pore size of GQDs composite membranes increased slightly from 3.4 nm (GQDs-EDA) to 3.8 nm (GQDs-PPD), while the dye rejection decreased slightly. This study provided an insight into the uniform self-assembly of GQDs into nanofilms to fine tune the nanochannel structure.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene-based separation membranes have attracted extensive attention and in-depth research since the birth of graphene and its derivatives owing to their special characteristics, such as frictionless surface transfer, easy modification and convenient assembly into films. Wherein, the two-dimensional (2D) graphene-based laminar membranes exploit the full advantages of the special morphology and properties of graphene, showing excellent controllable separation performance, which contain two types of transport channels: horizontal interlayer channel and vertical nanochannel [1,2]. A horizontal interlayer channel is an interlayer structure formed by two adjacent 2D nanosheets, that can be used for molecular sieve separation with fixed interlayer spacing. Precise adjustment of the interlayer spacing as tunable nanochannel pores has become the key to fabricate 2D laminar membranes, especially those assembled from non-porous 2D graphene. Vertical nanochannels include in-plane defects on 2D nanosheets, nano-pores generated by edge gaps between adjacent 2D nanosheets and inherent in-plane pores of 2D nanosheets [3]. In 2D laminar membranes, the membrane selectivity is mainly controlled by the size sieving mechanism [4]. The 2D lamellar membrane can trap solute molecules with molecular size larger than the interlayer spacing, in-plane defects or inherent pores of membranes, while small solutes can pass through. Adjusting the ratio between horizontal interlayer channels and vertical nanochannels can provide solute molecules with relatively less tortuous transport paths. For example, using a 2D material with a small flake size can create more vertical nanochannels to achieve higher permeability [5].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with the nanosheets less than 10 nm are one of the members of the graphene family, which not only has the inherent properties of graphene, but also possesses unique physical/chemical properties (e.g., quantum size, edge effect, excellent dispersion, chemical stability, etc.) [6–11]. GQDs display a typical zero-dimensional structure at the macroscopic level, whereas the single-layer GQDs display a typical two-dimensional nanosheet structure at the microscopic level. Due to their small nanometer size, the traditional film-forming methods for graphene and its derivatives, like layer-by-layer deposition [12], drop casting [13], vacuum filtration [14–16], spin coating [17], etc., are not applicable to GQDs films, because the supporting membranes are mostly ultrafiltration membrane, which present a large aperture (10–100 nm) and are prone to leakage. Therefore, GQDs are mainly used as nano-additives to prepare organic/inorganic nanomaterial composite membranes to improve their comprehensive performance [18–21]. However, the patterns for regulating transport channels of graphene laminar membranes, which are widely studied and relatively mature, are difficult to be realized at the nanoscale for GQDs. Hence, how to achieve efficient membrane formation of nanoscale GQDs and efficient construction of nanoscale separation channels is the core and key problem to expand the application of GQDs in the membrane field.

Electrospray is a technology in which the solution disperses into tiny droplets by overcoming the liquid surface tension under the action of a high voltage electric field, and then uniformly deposits on the substrate. In the electrospray process, all the tiny droplets carried with the same charge will repel each other, which can effectively prevent the agglomeration of droplets and make the droplets have stronger penetration in the air, thus making the electrospray technology have the characteristics of good dispersion and high purity [22–25]. This excellent and efficient dispersion property is especially suitable for the dispersion and uniform deposition of small particle size materials, which can significantly reduce the agglomeration phenomenon caused by the inherent high surface energy of small size nanomaterials, so that, the dispersion of nanomaterials and the thickness of functional layers can be easily and effectively controlled by reducing the heterogeneity of dispersion and the possibility of defects generation. Therefore, the concerned researches have extensively covered the field of surface engineering (e.g., surface modification [26–28], interface construction [29,30]), environmental remediation (e.g., catalytic degradation [31], dye desalination [32–34], oil/water separation [35,36] and seawater desalination [37–39]), medical engineering (e.g., drug loading [40], antibacterial [22]) and micro-nano thin membranes [23,24,41].

Based on the nanometer size, edge functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, etc.) and 2D structure at the micro-level, the GQDs can be intercalated with diamine monomers, forming covalent interactions with the edge functional groups. Therefore, the macroscopic GQDs composite membranes can be prepared by electrospray assisted self-assembly technology with the different diamine monomers as the bridging agents to tailor the interlayer nanochannels between GQDs nanosheets by means of covalent interactions. Then, the two types of transport pathways of GQDs composite membranes can be controlled simultaneously: vertical nanochannels are regulated by small GQDs nanosheets to provide more transport channels and shorten the vertical transport pathway, while the horizontal interlayer channels are controlled by introducing diamine monomer to regulate interlayer spacing.

Herein, we proposed a facile and effective method to fabricate the GQDs composite membranes based on electrospray assisted self-assembly method by incorporating diamine monomers between GQDs nanosheets in a simple one-step way. Four kinds of diamine monomers with different structures and molecular weights were chosen as the bridging agents to fine tune the nanochannels between GQDs nanosheets. The physicochemical structures of as-prepared GQDs composite membranes were further optimized by post-crosslinking. By regulating types of diamine monomers and post-crosslinking, the structures and properties of the GQDs composite membranes were investigated. Meanwhile, the nanochannel structure of the self-assembling GQDs membranes was further analyzed.

Single layer graphene oxide (GO, >98%) was provided by Soochow Hengqiu Graphene Technology Co. Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Piperazine (PIP, 86.14) and 1,5-pentanediamine (PDA, 102.18) were acquired from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA, 60.10), p-Phenylenediamine (PPD, 108.14), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) and Na2SO4 were bought from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Trimesoyl chloride (TMC) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). Rose Bengal (RB), Congo Red (CR), Direct Red 80 (DR) and n-hexane were acquired from Kermel Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Polysulfone membrane (PSF, MWCO∼30 kDa) and polytetrafluoroethylene membrane (PTFE, 0.22 μm) were obtained from Bishuiyuan Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China) and Labfilter Co. Ltd. (USA), respectively.

The GQDs were prepared via an improved mild oxidation cutting method reported in our previously work [42]. Typically, a mixture with GO aqueous solution (100 mL, 0.2 w/v%) and H2O2 solution (100.0 mL) was put into a three-necked flask and then treated under ultrasonic stripping for 2 h (25 °C, sealed condition). Then, the excess H2O2 solution in the mixture was removed by refluxing at 100 °C for 2 h under stirring conditions. Finally, the GQDs solution was obtained after filtering through PTFE membrane.

In this process, the GQDs was prepared by top-down oxidation cutting of GO. First, H2O2 was dissociated into hydroxyl radical (·OH). Then, this hydroxyl radical with strong oxidation effect would attack C–O bond to perform effectively oxidation cutting of the epoxy group on GO [42–44]. Finally, the GQDs (crystal plane spacing less than 0.24 nm, average size was 2.39 nm, diameter distribution ranged from 1 to 5 nm, Fig. S1) with abundant peripheral carboxyl groups were prepared and showed similar chemical composition to GO, which also showed good water dispersion (details were given in our previously work [42]).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the GQDs composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs composite membranes were prepared via electrospray assisted self-assembly method and the scheme of the membrane preparation process was shown in Fig. 1 , and chemical schematic diagrams of different diamine monomers (EDA, PDA and PPD) were shown in Fig. S2. Before the electrospray experiment, four kinds of pure diamine monomers (EDA, PIP, PDA, PPD; 0.5 mL) were uniformly dissolved in the GQDs solution (100 mL, GQDs concentration: 0.5 w/v%) to prepare the electrospray solution (diamine concentration: 0.5 v/v%), and the GQDs/diamine monomer aggregates were formed by electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bond after diamine monomer was added to GQDs solution, where the electrostatic attraction was generated between -COO- (GQD) and -NH3+ (diamine monomer) [19,45–47]. Then, the obtained electrospray solution was transferred to a syringe and the electrospray parameters were set as follows: the collector drum speed was 500 rpm, the flow rate was 4.0 mL h−1, the voltage was 25 kV, the distance between the needle tip and the collector drum was 12.5 cm, the temperature and relative humidity were set as 20 ± 5 °C and 25 ± 5%, respectively. Finally, under the coulombic repulsion forces, the electrospray solutions were disbursed as microdroplets, which were deposited onto the PSF substrate membrane (M0, Fig. S3), and covalent bonds were formed between the GQDs and diamine monomer after the evaporation of aqueous solution [48,49]. After 2.0 h, the GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers (EDA, PIP, PDA, PPD) were prepared and named as M1, M2, M3, M4, respectively.

In order to further improve the densification and repair the large defect holes on the surface of GQDs composite membrane to improve its rejection performance, the post-crosslinking with TMC was applied in the following experiment [42]. Specifically, the GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers prepared in the above step were immersed into TMC/n-hexane solution (0.3w/v%) for 2 min. Then, the post-crosslinked GQDs composite membranes were prepared after heat-treatment at 60 °C for 10 min in the oven. All the post-crosslinked GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers (EDA, PIP, PDA, PPD) were named as M1C, M2C, M3C and M4C, respectively.

The morphologies of the GQDs composite membranes were observed by field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan). The surface roughness of the GQDs composite membranes were characterized by atomic force microscope (AFM, Brooker Icon, USA). The distribution of surface elements of the GQDs composite membranes was detected by scanning electron microscope-energy dispersive spectrometer (SEM-EDS, UltimMax100, UK). The chemical structure and the element content of the GQDs composite membranes were analyzed by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher, USA), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, K-alpha, Thermo Fisher, USA) and Solid Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectrometer (Solid-NMR, AVANCE NEO 600 M, Bruker, Germany). The interlayer spacing between GQDs layers was studied by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 DISCOVER, BRUKER, Germany), and the structure schematic diagrams of diamine monomers between GQDs nanosheets in vertical and horizontal directions were studied by Materials Studio. The surface charge and water contact angle (WCA) were investigated by the solid surface zeta potential analyzer (SURPASS-3, Anton Paar, Austria) and contact angle meter (Kruss DSA-100, Germany), respectively. The pore size of the GQDs composite membranes were tested by BET pore size analyzer (BSD-PM2, China). All the samples were freeze-dried before testing.

A laboratory-made cross-flow filtration device (effective area: 7.1 cm2) was used to measure the separation performance of the prepared GQDs composite membranes at room temperature and at a 3 bar operating pressure. The mixture solution made by the different anionic dyes (CR, RB, DR; 0.1 g L−1; Table S1) and Na2SO4 (1.0 g L−1) was used to measure the separation performance of the GQDs composite membranes. Before the filtration experiment, the GQDs composite membranes were preloaded at 3 bar for 60 min to steady the membrane performance, and then the permeation flux (J) was calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)

V
T × A
where, J was the permeation flux (L·m−2·h−1); V, T and A were the volume of permeated water (L), the operating time (h) and the membrane effective area (m2), respectively.

The surface morphologies of the as-prepared GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers were shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that all these obtained GQDs composite membranes showed relatively dense surface structure with no obvious pores. For these four kind membranes, the M1, M2 and M3 membranes prepared with the EDA, PIP and PDA as the bridging agents were presented the similar smooth surface structure, which was different from the irregular stacking and convex structure of M4 membrane prepared with PPD as the bridging agent. This different phenomenon was due to the fact that the PPD containing the benzene ring occupied more space between GQDs nanosheets in both vertical and horizontal directions, resulting in disordered stacking of the GQD nanosheets (Fig. 4). After post-crosslinking, the surface structure of M1C, M2C and M3C membranes prepared with the EDA, PIP and PDA as the bridging agents was changed and showed a uniform convex vesicle structure with no obvious defect. These convex structures were typical polyamide vesicle structure generated by the polymerization reaction between residual diamine monomers and TMC during the post-crosslinking process. The structure and size of the microvesicles formed by different diamine monomers exhibited small variations, which were closely related to the structure and properties of the diamine monomers. Since the irregular convex surface of M4 membrane increased the reaction area with TMC, the M4C membrane prepared with PPD as the bridging agent formed an irregular and heterogeneous large convex vesicle structure after post-crosslinked by TMC [50,51]. This was also the difference brought by the unique structure of PPD to the GQDs composite membrane. In addition, the surface roughness of the series post-crosslinked GQDs composite membranes increased from 4.85 to 21.60 nm, which was corresponded to the surface morphology structure of the post-crosslinked composite membranes.

In order to further understand the elemental distribution of different diamine monomers on the surface of GQDs composite membrane, EDS characterization of N element was performed on the surface of M1C, M2C, M3C and M4C membranes and was shown in Fig. 2. The results showed that the N element was uniformly distributed on these four GQDs composite membranes, indicating the uniform reaction of GQDs with diamine monomers. Because the GQDs nanosheets were linked with each other with diamine monomer as the bridging agent to form the membrane separation layer, thus the uniformly distributed N element also indirectly reflected the relatively uniform distribution of GQDs nanosheets on the membrane surface with no obvious agglomeration.

The cross-section morphologies of the as-prepared GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers were shown in Fig. 3
. It can be seen that the cross-sections of all these four kind GQDs composite membranes were composed of spongy-like support layer and dense separation layer, and the thickness of the separation layer was varied with the different structure and molecular weight of diamine monomers. The separation layer thickness of the prepared GQDs composite membranes was less than 71 nm, indicating the formation of the relatively thin separation layer prepared by the electrospray assisted self-assembly method in this experiment. As the molecular weight of diamine monomer increased (EDA < PIP < PDA < PPD), the thickness of the obtained membrane separation layer was gradually increased from 48.5 nm for M1 to 61.7 nm for M4, and from 56.7 nm for M1C to 70.8 nm for M4C for the post-crosslinked membranes, which was closely related to the distribution of four diamine monomers between GQDs nanosheets in the separation layer (Fig. 4). Due to the differences in structure and molecular weight, these four diamine monomers occupied different inter spacing between the GQDs nanosheets in the separation layer. As shown in Fig. 4, the inter spacing between the GQDs nanosheets occupied by chain-like diamine monomers (EDA, PIP and PDA) was increased with the increase of chain length and molecular weight. Different from the PDA with the similar molecular weight, the PPD with the benzene ring structure occupied more inter spacing between the GQDs nanosheets and formed a more loose and thick separation layer, which was concerned to the relatively large volume and high steric hindrance of the benzene ring. Therefore, the molecular weight and structure of diamine monomer were together led to the difference in the thickness of separation layer for GQDs composite membranes. This kind controllable construction of the inter spacing of the GQDs composite membranes may help to achieve the controlled separation of substances with different molecular weights.

Fig. 5 a showed the FTIR spectra of the GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers. The absorption peak at 1408 cm−1 was the superimposed stretching vibration of C–N formed by the acylation reaction of GQDs with diamine monomer [42,52,53] and the polymerization reaction of remaining unreacted amine group in diamine monomer with TMC, while the superposition of multiple absorption peaks at 1657 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 was related to the CO stretching vibration of amide and carboxyl groups. This indicated that the GQDs composite separation layer with different diamine monomers was successfully constructed on the surface of the PSF substrate membranes. In addition, the particularly obvious absorption peak at 3226 cm−1 was caused by the superposition of –OH and –NH, where the stretching vibration of –OH corresponded to the inherent functional group of GQDs and the carboxyl group hydrolyzed by remaining acyl chloride of TMC, while the stretching vibration of –NH corresponded to the amide group. The two absorption peaks at 1244 and 1583 cm−1 were represented the stretching vibration of aromatic ether and benzene ring structures in PSF substrate membrane. A noteworthy point was the relatively weak peak intensity of M4C here as compared with other three membranes, which was related to the thicker surface separation layer of M4C membrane (Fig. 3).

Fig. 5b showed the surface XPS spectra of GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers. There were three distinct characteristic peaks appeared on the surface of these four GQDs composite membranes, representing C, N and O elements, respectively, while no obvious S element was detected, indicating that the surface of the PSF substrate membrane was covered with a defect-free dense separation layer. The high-resolution spectra of N1s, O1s and C1s of M1 was further confirmed that the acylation reaction occurred between GQDs and diamine monomer from Fig. S4. Meanwhile, the high-resolution spectra of O1s and N1s of the post-crosslinked M1C-M4C membranes were further confirmed the polymerization reaction of remaining unreacted amine group in diamine monomer with TMC from Fig. S5. In addition, Fig. 5c–f showed the high-resolution spectra of C1s of the post-crosslinked membranes, which showed the two peaks of OC–N at 287.6 eV and C–N at 285.8 eV, representing the typical polyamide structure [32]. In addition, the 13C NMR spectra of M2C were shown in Fig. S6, there were only obvious peaks at 150–180 ppm (carbonyls, aromatics) and 30 ppm (aliphatic), which were judged as the structure of polyamide and GQDs, and the aliphatic structure of PIP, respectively.

The surface charge of charged membrane is the direct factor that determines the electrostatic interaction between pollutants and membrane surface. Due to the different numbers and types of functional groups on the membrane surface and the degree of electrolysis, the membrane surface will show different charges in different pH solutions, which played an important role in the membrane separation process. According to the zeta potential measurements of these four GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers from Fig. 5g, all the prepared GQDs composite membranes showed negative zeta potential from pH = 3 to pH = 10 due to the existence of the carboxyl groups (Fig. 5a). This negatively charged membrane was expected to efficiently remove negatively charged solutes from wastewater solutions with different pH values. It was worth mentioning that the absolute value of GQDs composite membrane surface potential decreased with the increase of molecular weight of diamine monomers (M1C: −34.3 mV, M2C: −28.6 mV, M3C: −22.3 mV, M4C: −16.5 mV) as pH = 7, which might reduce the rejection of the solutes with negative charges.

The water contact angle was utilized to access the hydrophilicity of the GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers. As shown in Fig. 5h and i, the obtained membranes covered with the GQDs composite separation layer showed relatively lower water contact angles being less than 51° compared with the PSF substrate membrane (61.2°), indicating the excellent hydrophilicity. Among them, the water contact angles of M1-M4 membranes were smaller than those of post-crosslinked M1C-M4C membranes, which was mainly related to the existence of multiple hydrophilic groups (e.g., amide, carboxyl and hydroxyl) in the M1-M4 membranes, while the post-crosslinking mainly formed amide functional groups covering part of the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDs composite membranes, resulting in a slight increase in the contact angles of M1C-M4C membranes.

Based on the above analysis, we already knew that the covalent reactions between GQDs nanosheets and diamine monomers had been occurred, and the diamine monomers were successfully intercalated into the GQDs composite membranes. Thus, the link types of diamine monomers between GQDs nanosheets determined the tunability of nanochannels in horizontal and vertical directions of GQDs composite membranes. For each kind of GQDs composite membranes, covalent reactions can be assumed between parallel or perpendicular GQDs nanosheets, or both. If the diamine monomers only reacted with GQDs nanosheets in parallel, the interlayer width between GQDs nanosheets remained unchanged, which would give the same molecular sieving performance for GQDs composite membranes with a certain thickness. If the diamine monomers only reacted with GQDs nanosheets in perpendicular, the interlayer width between GQDs nanosheets would be changed with the structure of the intercalated diamine monomers, which would further realize the tunable of the molecular sieving performance of GQDs composite membranes. Otherwise, the diamine monomers reacted with GQDs nanosheets in both parallel and perpendicular, the nanochannels might be closely related to the three-dimensional space architecture of the GQDs nanosheets.

In order to further characterize the effect of diamine monomers on the nanochannel size of the GQDs composite membranes, the BET was employed to test the pore size distribution of the GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers. As shown in Fig. 6b, the pore size of GQDs composite membranes was increased gradually from 3.4 nm (M1C) to 3.8 nm (M4C) with the increase of the molecular weight of diamine monomers, while the pore size distribution of all GQDs composite membranes was mainly concentrated at 2–6 nm. When the diamine monomers as the bridging agents were intercalated between GQDs nanosheets in the membrane preparation process, the bridges between GQDs nanosheets in horizontal and vertical directions were built up according to the reaction sites (carboxyl group) on the peripheral of GQDs nanosheets. Due to the differences in structure and molecular weight, the various diamine monomers occupied different spacing between the GQDs nanosheets in horizontal and vertical directions (Fig. 4), which resulted in the increasing in the pore size of GQDs composite membranes with the increase of molecular weight of bridging agents. On the other hand, the diamine monomers with smaller molecular weight and size (EDA, PIP, Fig. 4) induced the GQDs to parallel arrangement, which corresponded to the XRD spectra of M1C and M2C in Fig. 6a.

From the above analysis, it can be concluded that the bridging agents with different structure and molecular weight can regulate the morphology, charge, hydrophilicity and pore size of GQDs composite membranes, which can further affect their separation performance.

In order to evaluate the separation performance of GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers, the dye desalination experiments were carried out on the as-prepared membrane, which used the CR/Na2SO4, RB/Na2SO4 and DR/Na2SO4 mixed systems of CR (696.7Da), RB (1017.6Da) and DR (1373.1Da) with Na2SO4 as the feed solutions [46,58]. Fig. 8 showed the separation performance of the obtained GQDs composite membranes for different dye/salt systems, respectively. Obviously, all the GQDs composite membranes showed relatively high dye rejection (>90%) and low Na2SO4 rejection (<35%), exhibiting practical dye desalination performance. Among them, both the dye and Na2SO4 rejections of post-crosslinked M1C-M4C membranes were higher than that of corresponding M1-M4 membranes, while the permeation flux was the opposite. This was mainly related to the fact that the loose surface structure of M1-M4 membranes was “sutured” by TMC during the post-crosslinking process, which brought about the increase of the surface density, the slight increase of the separation layer thickness and the decrease of the surface hydrophilicity for M1C-M4C membranes.

As can be seen from Fig. 8a and b, with the increase of the molecular weight of diamine monomers, the CR rejections of M1-M4 and post-crosslinked M1C-M4C membranes showed a gradually decrease trend in the separation of CR/Na2SO4 system，which was related to the different loose structures of the separation layers due to the fine-tuning nanochannels of diamine monomers between GQDs nanosheets. The larger molecular weight of the bridging agent led to the relatively looser structure of the GQDs separation layer, which increased the pore size of the GQDs composite membranes (Fig. 6b) and then decreased the CR rejection. Different from the varying of CR rejections, the permeation flux of the GQDs composite membranes tended to increase and then decrease with an increase in the molecular weight of diamine monomers. For the diamine monomers (EDA, PIP and PDA), the permeation flux of the GQDs composite membranes was increased with the increase of the pore size that caused by the increase of chain length and molecular weight. However, the PPD owning similar molecular weight with PDA occupied more inter spacing between the GQDs nanosheets due to the relatively large volume and high steric hindrance of the benzene ring, which increased the mass transfer resistance and decreased the permeation flux.

For the RB/Na2SO4 system, the RB rejection of M1 membrane (93.8%) was slightly higher than that of M2-M4 membranes (>90%), while the RB rejection of post-crosslinked membranes showed a gradually decrease trend (M1C (99.3%) > M2C (98.1%) > M3C (96.1%) > M4C (94.6%)) from Fig. 8c and d. Among them, M1C and M2C membranes can achieve a high rejection rate (>98.0%) for RB dye with a large molecular weight (1017.6 Da), which was related to their similar pore sizes (Fig. 6b). These results indicated that the nanochannel constructed by diamine monomers with GQDs nanosheets had preliminarily achieved efficient separation of RB/Na2SO4 system, exhibiting excellent dye desalination performance.

For the DR/Na2SO4 system, compared with the M1-M4 membranes (>94%), the DR rejection of post-crosslinked M1C-M4C membranes were higher than 98.7% from Fig. 8e and f, indicating that the obtained post-crosslinked membranes had achieved high DR rejections and high penetration of Na2SO4. In addition, M3C and M4C membranes showed excellent dye desalination performance in the DR/Na2SO4 system with large molecular dye compared with the small molecular dye (CR and BR), indicating that a controlled nanochannel could be constructed by regulating the inter spacing between GQDs nanosheets with large molecular weight diamine monomers, thus realizing the efficient separation of large molecular dyes (DR: 1373.1 Da).

In summary, the fine-tuning nanochannels of diamine monomers between GQDs nanosheets made the membrane separation layer with different loose structures, which endowed the GQDs composite membranes with effective dyes separation of different molecular weights, mainly manifested as the greater molecular weight of dyes corresponding with the higher membrane rejection. As shown in Fig. 8g, under the premise of the almost unchanged Na2SO4 rejection, the M1C membrane can achieve efficient separation of dyes with molecular weight over CR (696.7Da, >99.0%); the M2C membrane can achieve efficient separation of dyes with molecular weight over RB (1017.6Da, >98.0%), the M3C and M4C membranes can achieve efficient separation of dyes with molecular weight over DR (1373.1Da, >98.7%).

In order to further evaluate the stability and antifouling properties of GQDs composite membranes with different diamine monomers, the multi-cycle test of dye desalination was carried out by the cross-flow filtration system. Here, the mixture solutions made by three anionic dyes (CR, RB, DR; 0.1 g L−1) and Na2SO4 (1.0 g L−1) were utilized as the feed solution. For each cycle experiment, the GQDs composite membranes were run at 3 bar for 60 min to obtain the filtration data, and then the fouling membranes were washed by deionized water at 3 bar for 30 min. Fig. 9 showed the four-cycle filtration performance of post-crosslinked M1C-M4C membranes in dye desalination experiment. It can be seen that the dyes and Na2SO4 rejections of post-crosslinked M1C-M4C membranes remained relatively stable during the whole four-cycle tests despite a slight decrease in membrane permeation flux, which indicated the as-prepared M1C-M4C membranes possessing the excellent operation stability and antifouling performance.

From Fig. 9a, M1C membrane showed the similar dye rejection, Na2SO4 rejection and permeation flux for the three dye/salt solutions in the four-cycle tests. With the prolonging of the operation time, the rejection of the M1C membrane to three different molecular weight dyes and Na2SO4 basically remained unchanged, while the permeation flux showed a slow downward trend. These changes were related to the adsorption and deposition of dye molecules caused by concentration polarization, which was not completely removed by the simple hydraulic cleaning, resulting in a slow decrease in the permeation flux. The filtration performance of the M2C-M4C membranes to three dye/salt solutions also showed the similar trend to that of the M1C membrane from Fig. 9b, c and d. The difference was that the permeation flux of M2C-M4C membranes decreased with the increase of dye molecular weight, while the dyes and Na2SO4 rejections increased, which might be related to the high osmotic pressure caused by the higher dye rejection. In addition, with the prolonging of the operation time, the decline rate of permeation flux of M2C-M4C membranes slowed down with the increase of dye molecular weight, which was related to the larger nanochannels constructed by the intercalated diamine monomer with larger molecular weight between the GQDs nanosheets, thus enlarged the membrane pore size (Fig. 6b). However, the small dyes were more easily embedded into the larger nanochannels, resulting in irreversible fouling, which was difficult to remove by the simple hydraulic cleaning and lead to a higher decline rate of permeation flux. Nevertheless, a small amount of dye residue in the nanochannels had little influence on the rejection of the mixture system, which could ensure the excellent dye desalination performance of the M1C-M4C membranes.

Furthermore, Fig. 10 showed the comparation of the dye desalination performance of GQDs composite membranes with other dye desalination membranes reported in the literatures [2,12,16,47,59–71]. As can be seen that the as-prepared M1C-M3C membranes presented the relatively high salt/dye selectivity and permeability, which could be comparable to those of membranes reported in literatures, indicating the advantages of GQDs composite membranes in the application of dye desalination.

GQDs composite membranes were successfully prepared via the electrospray assisted self-assembly method by intercalating diamine monomers between GQDs nanosheets. Diamine monomers with different structures and molecular weights as bridging agents changed the loose structures of the separation layers due to the fine-tuning nanochannels of diamine monomers between GQDs nanosheets, which endowed the GQDs composite membranes with the adjustable pore size and better hydrophilicity, further affecting their dye desalination performance. With the increase of the molecular weight of diamine monomers, the pore size of GQDs composite membranes increased slightly from 3.4 nm (M1C) to 3.8 nm (M4C), while the dye rejection decreased slightly. Under the premise of the almost unchanged Na2SO4 rejection, the M1C membrane can achieve efficient separation of dyes with molecular weight over CR (696.7Da, >99.0%); the M2C membrane can achieve efficient separation of dyes with molecular weight over RB (1017.6Da, >98.0%), the M3C and M4C membranes can achieve efficient separation of dyes with molecular weight over DR (1373.1Da, >98.7%). These results demonstrated the potential application in treating saline dye wastewater for environmental protection, and provided an insight into the uniform self-assembly of GQDs into nanofilms to fine tune the nanochannel structure.

Hailiang Liu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yumin Sun: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Shiyun Cheng: Methodology. Yang Qin: Writing – review & editing. Xianshe Feng: Investigation. Qinglin Huang: Software. Kaikai Chen: Writing – review & editing.
